content.text: What is a Multi-Tenant App Registration?

A multi-tenant app registration is an Azure AD / Microsoft Entra ID application that can be used by users and administrators from any Azure AD tenant — not just the tenant where the app was registered. This is the foundation of SaaS applications built on Microsoft's identity platform: an ISV registers the app once in their own tenant, and customer organizations consent to use it in their own tenants.

The core identity objects: one App Registration (in the publisher tenant) with a globally unique appId, and one Service Principal (created on consent) in each customer tenant. The appId is the same everywhere; the objectId of the service principal differs per tenant.
signInAudience Values
The signInAudience property on the app registration controls which identities can sign in. Choose based on your target audience:
signInAudience Value
Who Can Sign In
Authority URL
AzureADMyOrg
Users in the publisher tenant only (single-tenant)
https://login.microsoftonline.com/<tenantId>
AzureADMultipleOrgs
Users in any Azure AD work/school tenant
https://login.microsoftonline.com/common
AzureADandPersonalMicrosoftAccount
Work/school accounts + personal Microsoft accounts (MSA)
https://login.microsoftonline.com/common
PersonalMicrosoftAccount
Personal MSA only (Xbox, Outlook.com, etc.)
https://login.microsoftonline.com/consumers
Creating a Multi-Tenant App Registration
Via Azure Portal
Go to Microsoft Entra ID → App registrations → New registration.
Enter a display name.
Under Supported account types, select 'Accounts in any organizational directory (Any Azure AD directory - Multitenant)'.
Enter a redirect URI (e.g. https://myapp.contoso.com/auth/callback).
Click Register.
Note the Application (client) ID — this is the appId used in authorization requests.
Via PowerShell (Graph API)
How Consent Works in Multi-Tenant Apps

When a user from a customer organization first signs in to a multi-tenant app, Microsoft Entra ID creates a service principal object in the customer's tenant and records the consent grant. Consent can be given by an individual user (for delegated permissions) or by an admin on behalf of the whole organization (for application permissions and sensitive delegated permissions).
Admin Consent URL Pattern

# Construct the admin consent URL to send to customer admins
# Replace CLIENT_ID with your app's appId

CLIENT_ID='<your-app-client-id>'
REDIRECT_URI='https%3A%2F%2Fapp.contoso.com%2Fadmin-consent-callback'
STATE='csrf-protection-value'

echo "https://login.microsoftonline.com/common/adminconsent?"
echo "  client_id=${CLIENT_ID}"
echo "  &redirect_uri=${REDIRECT_URI}"
echo "  &state=${STATE}"

# When the admin lands on this URL, they see the permissions your app requests
# and grant consent for their entire organization.
# After consent, Entra ID creates a service principal in their tenant.
Checking if a Tenant Has Consented

# In the publisher tenant — check which tenants have a service principal for your app
Connect-MgGraph -Scopes 'Application.Read.All'

$appId = '<your-app-client-id>'

# Get the home service principal
$homeSp = Get-MgServicePrincipal -Filter "appId eq '$appId'"
Write-Host "Home SP object ID: $($homeSp.Id)"

# Note: service principals in CUSTOMER tenants are not visible from your tenant.
# To check customer-side consent, call the Graph API from the customer tenant context,
# or check your app's sign-in logs for the customer tenant's tid claim.

# List OAuth2 permission grants in YOUR tenant for your own SP
$grants = Get-MgServicePrincipalOauth2PermissionGrant -ServicePrincipalId $homeSp.Id
foreach ($g in $grants) {
    Write-Host "Scope: $($g.Scope) | Principal: $($g.PrincipalId) | Consent: $($g.ConsentType)"
}
App Roles for Multi-Tenant Apps

App roles let you define permission levels that customer admins can assign to their users or groups. Roles are defined once on the app registration and are available in every tenant where the app is consented.

Connect-MgGraph -Scopes 'Application.ReadWrite.All'

$appId = '<your-app-client-id>'
$app   = Get-MgApplication -Filter "appId eq '$appId'"

# Add two app roles: Reader and Admin
$newRoles = @(
    @{
        Id              = [System.Guid]::NewGuid().ToString()
        AllowedMemberTypes = @('User', 'Group')
        Description     = 'Can read data in Contoso SaaS App'
        DisplayName     = 'Reader'
        IsEnabled       = $true
        Value           = 'Reader'
    },
    @{
        Id              = [System.Guid]::NewGuid().ToString()
        AllowedMemberTypes = @('User', 'Group')
        Description     = 'Full admin access in Contoso SaaS App'
        DisplayName     = 'Admin'
        IsEnabled       = $true
        Value           = 'Admin'
    }
)

Update-MgApplication -ApplicationId $app.Id -AppRoles $newRoles
Write-Host 'App roles added. Customer admins can now assign these roles to their users.'
Token Validation in Multi-Tenant Apps

When your app uses the /common endpoint, the issuer claim (iss) in received tokens will contain the signing tenant's ID, not a fixed value. You must validate the tid (tenant ID) claim to ensure you only accept tokens from tenants you expect.

# Python example using msal and PyJWT for token validation
# pip install msal PyJWT cryptography
import jwt
import msal
import requests

# List of customer tenant IDs your app has onboarded
ALLOWED_TENANT_IDS = {
    'aaaabbbb-0000-1111-2222-ccccddddeeee',  # Customer A
    'ffffgggg-3333-4444-5555-hhhhiiiijjjj',  # Customer B
}

def validate_multi_tenant_token(id_token: str, client_id: str) -> dict:
    # Decode header to get kid
    header = jwt.get_unverified_header(id_token)

    # Fetch OIDC discovery + JWKS for the specific tenant in the token
    unverified = jwt.decode(id_token, options={'verify_signature': False})
    tid = unverified.get('tid')

    if tid not in ALLOWED_TENANT_IDS:
        raise ValueError(f'Token from unexpected tenant: {tid}')

    # Fetch JWKS for this tenant
    jwks_uri = f'https://login.microsoftonline.com/{tid}/discovery/v2.0/keys'
    jwks = requests.get(jwks_uri).json()
    signing_key = jwt.algorithms.RSAAlgorithm.from_jwk(
        next(k for k in jwks['keys'] if k['kid'] == header['kid'])
    )

    # Verify signature, audience, issuer
    claims = jwt.decode(
        id_token,
        key=signing_key,
        algorithms=['RS256'],
        audience=client_id,
        issuer=f'https://login.microsoftonline.com/{tid}/v2.0',
    )
    return claims
Terraform: Multi-Tenant App Registration
Common Issues
Error / Symptom
Cause
Fix
AADSTS650052: app needs access to a resource not listed
App requests a permission not declared in requiredResourceAccess
Add the missing resource access to the app manifest before requesting consent
Token from unexpected tenant accepted by app
App does not validate the tid claim
Add tenant ID allow-list validation before processing the token
Admin consent URL redirects to error after granting
redirect_uri in admin consent URL not registered in the app
Add the admin consent callback URI to the app's registered redirect URIs
Service principal not found in customer tenant
Customer admin has not yet consented
Send the /adminconsent URL to the customer tenant admin
App roles not visible in customer tenant
App roles defined after the service principal was created
Customer admin must re-consent after you add app roles; or update the existing SP
Client Credentials Flow for Multi-Tenant Daemon Apps

Server-to-server daemon applications use the client credentials flow (no user sign-in). In multi-tenant scenarios the daemon authenticates using the /common endpoint for token acquisition, but must specify which customer tenant's resources it is accessing.
For daemon apps, admin consent must be granted in each customer tenant before the app can obtain tokens for that tenant's resources. There is no user-delegated flow to grant consent interactively.
Daemon Token Acquisition (Python MSAL)

# pip install msal
import msal

# Publisher app credentials
CLIENT_ID     = '<your-app-client-id>'
CLIENT_SECRET = '<your-app-client-secret>'

# Target customer tenant — each customer requires separate token acquisition
CUSTOMER_TENANT_ID = 'aaaabbbb-0000-1111-2222-ccccddddeeee'

# Authority must target the specific customer tenant (not /common) for client credentials
authority = f'https://login.microsoftonline.com/{CUSTOMER_TENANT_ID}'

app = msal.ConfidentialClientApplication(
    client_id=CLIENT_ID,
    client_credential=CLIENT_SECRET,
    authority=authority,
)

# Acquire token for Microsoft Graph in the customer's tenant
result = app.acquire_token_for_client(
    scopes=['https://graph.microsoft.com/.default']
)

if 'access_token' in result:
    print(f'Token acquired for tenant {CUSTOMER_TENANT_ID}')
    # Use result['access_token'] to call Graph API on behalf of the customer tenant
else:
    print(f'Error: {result.get("error_description")}')
    # Common error: AADSTS700016 — admin consent not yet granted in customer tenant
Managing the App Manifest

The app manifest is the JSON representation of the app registration's full configuration. It is the source of truth for features like app roles, optional claims, known client applications, and identifier URIs that are not fully exposed in the portal UI.
Downloading and Updating the Manifest
Publisher Verification

Microsoft Entra ID enforces publisher verification for multi-tenant apps that request sensitive permissions. An unverified publisher causes the consent screen to show a warning ('Unverified'). Verification requires linking the app registration to a Microsoft Partner Network (MPN) account.
In the Azure Portal, navigate to App registrations → your app → Branding and properties.
Under Publisher domain, add and verify a custom domain that matches your company website.
Optionally complete MPN publisher verification for the highest trust level — shows 'Verified' badge on the consent screen.
Verified publisher status is required for apps requesting certain high-privilege permissions (e.g. Mail.Read, Files.ReadWrite.All) to avoid consent-blocking policies in customer tenants.
Publisher verification also enables your app to appear in the Azure AD Enterprise Apps gallery (if published).
KQL: Auditing Multi-Tenant App Consent Events
Consent Grants in Your Publisher Tenant

// Track admin and user consent grants for your multi-tenant app
AuditLogs
| where TimeGenerated > ago(90d)
| where OperationName in ('Consent to application', 'Add delegated permission grant',
                          'Add app role assignment to service principal')
| extend AppName  = tostring(TargetResources[0].displayName)
| extend GrantedTo = tostring(TargetResources[0].modifiedProperties
    | where Name == 'ConsentType' | project NewValue)[0]
| extend Actor = InitiatedBy.user.userPrincipalName
| project TimeGenerated, OperationName, AppName, Actor, Result
| order by TimeGenerated desc
Sign-ins Across Customer Tenants

// Sign-ins to your multi-tenant app — grouped by customer tenant
SignInLogs
| where TimeGenerated > ago(30d)
| where AppId == '<your-app-client-id>'
| summarize
    TotalSignIns = count(),
    UniqueUsers  = dcount(UserId),
    Failures     = countif(ResultType != 0)
    by ResourceTenantId, bin(TimeGenerated, 1d)
| order by TimeGenerated desc, TotalSignIns desc
Security Best Practices for Multi-Tenant Apps
Practice
Why It Matters
How to Implement
Validate tid claim in every token
Prevents token substitution attacks from unexpected tenants
Maintain an allowlist of onboarded tenant IDs; reject all others
Use certificate credentials, not client secrets
Certificates are private-key-only; secrets are symmetric and easier to leak
Store cert private key in Azure Key Vault; use X.509 credential on app registration
Request minimum permissions
Over-permissioned multi-tenant apps create large blast radius in customer tenants
Start with User.Read; escalate permissions only when features require them
Complete publisher verification
Prevents consent-screen warnings and consent-blocking policies
Verify publisher domain and complete MPN verification
Implement incremental consent
Avoids alarming customers with a large permission list on first sign-in
Request basic scopes on first auth; request additional scopes when features are used
Rotate client secrets before expiry
Expired secrets cause all customer sign-ins to fail simultaneously
Set 90-day rotation cadence; use Key Vault + managed identity for auto-rotation
Summary
A multi-tenant app registration has signInAudience set to AzureADMultipleOrgs (or wider), enabling users from any Entra ID tenant to sign in.
One App Registration lives in the publisher tenant; a separate Service Principal is created in each customer tenant on consent.
The appId is globally unique and constant across all tenants; the service principal objectId differs per tenant.
Customer admins grant consent via the /adminconsent URL, creating the service principal and recording permission grants in their tenant.
App roles defined on the registration are available for assignment in all customer tenants after consent.
Always validate the tid claim in received tokens and reject tokens from tenants your app has not onboarded.
